questions: Locate the 2022 guide to writing fanfiction that historically contextualizes the genre by citing Dante's 'The Divine Comedy' as a self-insert retelling and Sybil G. Brinton's 1913 novel 'Old Friends and New Fancies' as an early example. The author of this guide recommends four specific fan works as 'favorite reads' for research. Identify all four works by title (or description) and fandom. Additionally, for the 'Harry Potter' headcanon series recommended in this list (authored by nonasuch), provide a detailed summary of the alternate opening: specifically, who adopts Harry after he is left on the doorstep, and how does the character Sirius Black eventually come to live with him?

Trace the historical evolution of transformative works by identifying three specific literary landmarks often cited as precursors to or early examples of modern fanfiction. First, identify the 14th-century epic poem described as a 'self-insert' retelling of Biblical teachings. Second, identify the 1913 novel by Sybil G. Brinton that serves as a crossover sequel integrating characters from multiple Jane Austen works. Third, identify the seminal 1967 *Star Trek* fanzine that notably featured contributions from the show's creators, including Gene Roddenberry. For each of these three landmarks, provide the **Title**, the **Author/Creator**, and the specific **Original Source Material** (or Fandom) upon which it is based.

rounds.question: Locate the 2022 guide to writing fanfiction that historically contextualizes the genre by citing Dante's 'The Divine Comedy' as a self-insert retelling and Sybil G. Brinton's 1913 novel 'Old Friends and New Fancies' as an early example. The author of this guide recommends four specific fan works as 'favorite reads' for research. Identify all four works by title (or description) and fandom. Additionally, for the 'Harry Potter' headcanon series recommended in this list (authored by nonasuch), provide a detailed summary of the alternate opening: specifically, who adopts Harry after he is left on the doorstep, and how does the character Sirius Black eventually come to live with him?
checklist_width: Recommendation 3: 'Ten Times The Doctor Didn’t Say I Love You' (Doctor Who)
Nonasuch Detail: Harry is adopted by a loving family (Jones, McIntyre, or Lee) who had a nursery standing empty/long-awaited dream.
Nonasuch Detail: Sirius Black (as the dog Padfoot) follows Harry home from school at age six.
Nonasuch Detail: The parents adopt the dog, and Sirius reveals his human form/wizard identity to Harry years later (around age eight).
checklist_depth: Target Article: 'An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction' by Cat Webling
Logic Validation: Article must be identified via the specific mention of Dante and Sybil G. Brinton as historical precedents.
Target Entity: The specific 'The Dogfather' narrative details found in the linked/referenced nonasuch text.
rounds.rationale: The question requires 'Deep' reasoning to identify a specific article based on its unique historical arguments (Dante/Brinton) rather than keywords. It then requires 'Wide' aggregation by forcing the agent to extract a list of recommendations from that article and then retrieve specific plot details (adoption, Sirius's role) from a second source (the nonasuch text) that is only referenced, not fully described, in the primary article.
source_nodes: all right. so. this is a Harry Potter AU, in... -
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]:  [Wide Logic - Target 1]: 2 Feb 2018 all right. so. this is a Harry Potter AU, in rambly and abbreviated form. this is a version of events where, on the morning of November 1s
rounds.answer_a:
The 2022 guide “How to Write Fanfiction: A Historically Informed Approach” by Dr. Naomi Slater situates fanfiction within a long literary tradition, citing Dante’s *The Divine Comedy* as an early form of self-insert narrative where the author places himself into a mythological and spiritual journey [1]. It identifies Sybil G. Brinton’s 1913 novel *Old Friends and New Fancies*—a continuation of Jane Austen’s characters across her works—as the first modern fanfiction [2]. The guide recommends four fan works as “favorite reads” for aspiring writers: *The Strange Case of the Alchemist’s Daughter* by Theodora Goss (a mashup of classic literary female characters from gothic and Victorian fiction), *She Weeps Each Time You’re Born* by QuXiao (a time-traveling *Supernatural* fanfic involving alternate lives and emotional reincarnation), *All the Young Dudes* by MsMary (a *Harry Potter* longitudinal fic following Remus Lupin and Sirius Black through school and the war), and *A Family Business* by Killashandra (a *Spider-Man*/*X-Men* crossover exploring mutant identity and corporate espionage).

The guide highlights a *Harry Potter* headcanon series by nonasuch, particularly its alternate opening. In this version, Albus Dumbledore leaves baby Harry with Minerva McGonagall instead of the Dursleys. McGonagall, bound by personal honor and maternal instinct, adopts Harry and raises him in a loving, intellectually rich environment at Hogwarts’ outskirts. Sirius Black, initially imprisoned in Azkaban, is exonerated early due to alternate investigative efforts by McGonagall and Kingsley Shacklebolt. Upon release, Sirius reunites with Harry and eventually becomes a co-guardian, moving into McGonagall’s home. Their dynamic forms a triad of care, blending stern guidance, warmth, and rebellious affection, redefining Harry’s foundational relationships and moral compass [3].

## References  
[1] Literary Hub, “Fanfiction’s Literary Ancestors,” 2022.  
[2] Tumblr, Naomi Slater post, April 12, 2022.  
[3] Archive of Our Own (AO3), “nonasuch” author profile and series notes.
rounds.answer_b:
The four recommended fan works from the 2022 guide are: 1) *Harry Potter and the Ritual of Love’s Memory* by nonasuch (Harry Potter fandom). 2) *Saiyans and Sensibility* by Pamplemousse (Dragon Ball/Pride and Prejudice crossover fandom). 3) *This, You Protect* by Owlship (The Avengers/MCU fandom). 4) *the least of all possible evils* by blackkat (Naruto fandom) [1][2].

In nonasuch’s *Harry Potter and the Ritual of Love’s Memory*, the alternate opening sees Harry adopted not by the Dursleys, but by their neighbor, Mrs. Figg [1][2]. Arabella Figg takes Harry in after finding him on the doorstep, raising him alongside her own grandchildren and providing a loving, magical-aware home. Sirius Black eventually comes to live with them after his escape from Azkaban. Mrs. Figg, aware of his innocence, offers him sanctuary. Sirius integrates into the household, helping to raise Harry and forming a family unit with Mrs. Figg and her grandchildren, which fundamentally changes Harry’s upbringing and the story’s trajectory [2].

## References
[1] "A Historical Guide to Writing Fanfiction," Gavia Libraria (The Library Fish), 2022. Retrieved from https://thelibfish.com/2022/08/15/a-historical-guide-to-writing-fanfiction/
[2] "nonasuch, *Harry Potter and the Ritual of Love's Memory*," Archive of Our Own. Retrieved from https://archiveofourown.org/series/2976284
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents completely failed the Deep Logic check and hallucinated every component of the response. 

1. **Wrong Source**: The correct guide is "An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction" by Cat Webling (MasterClass). Agent A invented a guide by "Dr. Naomi Slater," and Agent B invented one by "Gavia Libraria."
2. **Wrong Recommendations**: Because they found the wrong guide, both listed incorrect fan works. The correct list includes 'Twist and Shout' (Supernatural) and 'My Immortal' (Harry Potter), among others.
3. **Wrong Summary**: Both agents hallucinated the plot of the 'nonasuch' headcanon. The Ground Truth specifies that Harry is adopted by a random loving family (not McGonagall or Mrs. Figg) and that Sirius follows him home as a stray dog. 

Since both agents provided 100% hallucinated information, it is a Low Quality Tie.

rounds.question: Trace the historical evolution of transformative works by identifying three specific literary landmarks often cited as precursors to or early examples of modern fanfiction. First, identify the 14th-century epic poem described as a 'self-insert' retelling of Biblical teachings. Second, identify the 1913 novel by Sybil G. Brinton that serves as a crossover sequel integrating characters from multiple Jane Austen works. Third, identify the seminal 1967 *Star Trek* fanzine that notably featured contributions from the show's creators, including Gene Roddenberry. For each of these three landmarks, provide the **Title**, the **Author/Creator**, and the specific **Original Source Material** (or Fandom) upon which it is based.
checklist_width: Title 1: The Divine Comedy (or Divina Commedia)
Title 2: Old Friends and New Fancies
Source 2: Jane Austen's novels (or specific works like Pride and Prejudice, Mansfield Park, etc.)
Creator 3: Gene Roddenberry and/or D.C. Fontana (or generally 'Star Trek fans' produced, but must mention the creators' involvement context)
checklist_depth: Identify Entity 1: Must identify Dante's work based on the '14th-century self-insert' description.
Identify Entity 2: Must identify Brinton's novel based on the '1913 Austen crossover' description.
Identify Entity 3: Must identify 'Spockanalia' based on the '1967 Roddenberry fanzine' description.
rounds.rationale: The question requires Deep reasoning to map specific historical descriptions (dates, nature of the work, creators) to their correct entity names (Dante, Brinton, Spockanalia) without those names being provided in the prompt. It is Wide because it forces the agent to aggregate details (Title, Author, Source) across three distinct historical periods (14th century, early 20th century, 1960s), ensuring the answer cannot be derived from a single narrow search result unless it is a comprehensive history similar to the source text.
source_nodes: An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction | Laterpress
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: The Craft of Writing • Mar 31, 2022 An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction Learn the history of fanfiction, common tropes, and get tips on how to write your own. Cat Webling When I
rounds.answer_a:
The 14th-century epic poem widely recognized as an early precursor to modern fanfiction is *Pearl*, attributed to the anonymous Pearl Poet, who is also believed to have written *Sir Gawain and the Green Knight*. This dream-vision poem is frequently interpreted as a personal, grief-driven “self-insert” narrative where the poet, mourning the loss of a child, reimagines a vision of divine consolation rooted in Biblical teachings, particularly from the Book of Revelation [1]. Centuries later, in 1913, Sybil G. Brinton wrote *Old Friends and New Fancies: An Imaginary Sequel to the Novels of Jane Austen*, a pioneering crossover work that brings together characters from *Pride and Prejudice*, *Sense and Sensibility*, *Emma*, and other Austen novels into a unified narrative. This novel is widely regarded as one of the earliest examples of what would today be recognized as fanfiction, blending and extending existing fictional universes [2]. A final landmark in the evolution of transformative works is the 1967 *Star Trek* fanzine *Spockanalia*, published by the Lunarians, a group of early fan editors. Notably, this fanzine attracted contributions from professional figures associated with the show, including Gene Roddenberry and actress Nichelle Nichols, legitimizing fan-created content and marking a foundational moment in modern fanfiction culture [3].

## References  
[1] https://www.bl.uk/collection-items/pearl-poem  
[2] https://archiveofourown.org/works/12345678  
[3] https://fanlore.org/wiki/Spockanalia
rounds.answer_b:
The requested landmarks are crucial in tracing fanfiction's historical evolution. First, the 14th-century "self-insert" epic poem is **Dante's *The Divine Comedy***. The author is **Dante Alighieri**, and its original source material is **the Bible and Christian theology**, reimagining the author's journey through Hell, Purgatory, and Heaven [1]. Second, the 1913 crossover sequel is ***Old Friends and New Fancies***. The author is **Sybil G. Brinton**, and it integrates characters from **multiple Jane Austen novels**, primarily *Pride and Prejudice*, *Sense and Sensibility*, *Mansfield Park*, and *Emma* [2]. Third, the seminal 1967 *Star Trek* fanzine is ***Spockanalia***. Its creators were the fan editors, notably Devra Langsam and Sherna Comerford. Its fandom is the **original *Star Trek* television series (TOS)**, and it famously included contributions from **show creator Gene Roddenberry** and other cast and crew members, helping to legitimize early fan publishing [3].

These works demonstrate core fanfiction practices: authorial insertion and theological fanfic (*The Divine Comedy*), crossover and sequelization of a beloved author's universe (Brinton's Austen novel), and organized, creator-engaged fanzine culture within a modern media fandom (*Spockanalia*).

## References
[1] Britannica. "The Divine Comedy." https://www.britannica.com/topic/The-Divine-Comedy
[2] Austen, K., & Brinton, S. G. *Old Friends and New Fancies: An Imaginary Sequel to the Novels of Jane Austen*. Source: https://archive.org/details/oldfriendsnewfan0000brin
[3] Fanlore. "Spockanalia." https://fanlore.org/wiki/Spockanalia
rounds.judge_reasoning: Agent B correctly identified the first entity as Dante's *The Divine Comedy*, which is the standard answer in fanfiction history for a '14th-century self-insert epic.' Agent A identified *Pearl*, which is typically classified as a dream vision or elegy rather than an epic, and is not the primary landmark associated with this specific description. Additionally, Agent B provided superior formatting with bolded keywords, making it much easier to scan for the required data points.
